always.exam: Observation & posture (gait, lateral shift, lumbar lordosis)
always.why: Baseline for every lumbar assessment; screens antalgic gait, lateral shift, muscle guarding.
always.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens deformity, lateral shift, gait pattern

always.exam: Active lumbar ROM (flexion, extension, side-flexion, rotation) with symptom response
always.why: Establishes range, willingness to move, and directional pain/symptom response (centralisation vs peripheralisation).
always.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens restriction pattern and directional preference

always.exam: Passive PA spring test / segmental palpation (L1-L5, SI joints)
always.why: Localises the symptomatic segment and differentiates segmental stiffness/hypermobility from soft-tissue findings.

add.exam: Neurological screen (dermatomes/myotomes/reflexes L2-S1) + SLR/slump neurodynamic testing
add.why: Leg pain extending below the knee crosses the radiculopathy threshold; neural tension testing and a full neuro screen are required to confirm/exclude a nerve-root source.

add.exam: Walking tolerance / extension provocation vs flexion relief testing
add.why: A claudication-type pattern (worse with standing/walking, relieved by flexion/sitting) raises probability of central/lateral recess stenosis.

add.exam: SIJ provocation cluster (distraction, compression, thigh thrust, Gaenslen's)
add.why: Localised SI-joint/buttock pain without a clear lumbar-dominant pattern warrants the Laslett cluster; 3+ positives materially raises SIJ probability.

add.exam: Kemp's test + prone instability test + stork test
add.why: Extension-provoked pain raises probability of facet loading, segmental instability, or pars stress; this cluster differentiates the three.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes facet syndrome, segmental instability, spondylolysis

add.exam: Repeated-movement / directional-preference examination (McKenzie press-ups vs knee-to-chest) + slump test
add.why: Flexion-provoked pain with possible centralisation warrants directional-preference testing to characterise a discogenic pattern.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes discogenic pattern and directional preference

add.exam: Bilateral stork test + consider imaging if positive
add.why: A young athlete with extension-provoked unilateral low back pain is the classic spondylolysis presentation; stork test is the key clinical screen.

add.exam: Detailed myotomal + reflex + dermatomal screen (L4/L5/S1) with functional heel/toe walk
add.why: A reported foot drop suggests significant L4/L5 motor involvement; objective confirmation and monitoring for progression is required.

add.exam: Full bilateral neurological screen + cauda equina safety-net questions (bladder/bowel/saddle)
add.why: Bilateral leg symptoms can indicate central stenosis or, rarely, evolving cauda equina — always re-screen safety-netting questions.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Excludes cauda equina; confirms/excludes central stenosis pattern

supporting.why: Quantifies baseline disability for goal-setting and re-assessment.

supporting.exam: Palpation (PA central, unilateral facet/mammary, SI joint)
supporting.why: Localises tender structures to corroborate the segmental and SIJ findings above.

supporting.exam: Imaging (only if red flag, progressive neuro deficit, or suspected spondylolisthesis/pars fracture)
supporting.why: Imaging is not routine for mechanical low back pain; reserve for red flags, progressive deficit, or suspected structural/bony pathology.
supporting.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms structural pathology when clinically indicated
